# Optimization of antidepressant treatment by pharmacogenomics: a case report

[*BMC Psychiatry*](/)
**volume 25**, Article number: 34 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

1805 Accesses

1 Citations

5 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4/metrics)

## Abstract

### Background

Few new psychiatric drugs have entered the market in recent decades; in contrast, the number of drugs carrying pharmacogenomic labels continues to increase. For the foreseeable future, the advancement of psychiatry and drug therapy may hinge on personalized treatment. Currently, antipsychotic or antidepressant choices rely heavily on the clinical experience of psychiatrists and potentially lengthy iterative trials. During these trials, the clinical response to treatment in acutely depressed patients can be assessed only after several weeks of exposure to the drug. Although pharmacogenomic testing has been used in clinical care for several years, most Chinese clinicians struggle to utilize the information accurately, resulting in expensive tests that provide little real benefit to patients. Here, we demonstrate how to combine the results of pharmacogenomic testing to develop an individualized treatment plan. Our goal is to find the optimal medication regimen and dosage for the patient in the shortest possible time, control symptoms as soon as possible, and predict adverse drug reactions. This approach aims to offer a practical therapeutic idea for clinical practice.

### Case presentation

We present the case of a 27-year-old female patient experiencing a relapse of depression. Despite previous attempts with empiric medication, her symptoms remained uncontrolled, leading to exacerbation and drug withdrawal reactions. Utilizing the results of pharmacogenetic testing, we crafted an individualized treatment plan, resulting in rapid remission without any adverse drug reactions.

### Conclusion

Recognizing the complexity of antidepressant response, our patients aim to improve their understanding, as well as that of other healthcare providers, by undergoing pharmacogenomics testing. This enhances the credibility of their medication choices. While pharmacogenomics is just one aspect considered in selecting a treatment regimen for depression, it remains a valuable tool for increasing credibility and mitigating potential adverse events.

[Peer Review reports](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4/peer-review)

## Background

With the global rise in the burden of non-communicable diseases (NCDs), depression has emerged as a significant mental health concern, particularly in many low- and middle-income countries, where it contributes substantially to the overall health burden [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR1 "Burden of disease scenarios. For 204 countries and territories, 2022–2050: a forecasting analysis for the global burden of disease study 2021. Lancet. 2024;403(10440):2204–56.")]. The recurrence rate of depression is as high as 50–85%, with approximately 50% of patients relapsing within two years of the onset of the illness [[2](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR2 "Weissman MM. Pursuing the epidemiology and familial risks of depression and developing an evidence based psychotherapy. Psychiatry Res. 2022;317:114754.")]. Although antidepressant medications have been shown to be effective in alleviating depression, their use is often complicated by delayed clinical improvement and side effects [[3](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR3 "Binder EB, Holsboer F. Pharmacogenomics and antidepressant drugs. Ann Med. 2006;38(2):82–94.")]. The response rate to initial antidepressant treatment is approximately 50%, while the complete remission rate is around 37% [[4](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR4 "Rush AJ, Trivedi MH, Wisniewski SR, Nierenberg AA, Stewart JW, Warden D, Niederehe G, Thase ME, Lavori PW, Lebowitz BD, et al. Acute and longer-term outcomes in depressed outpatients requiring one or several treatment steps: a STAR*D report. Am J Psychiatry. 2006;163(11):1905–17.")]. This means that many patients may experience inadequate management of depressive symptoms and/or adverse drug reactions for extended periods before the appropriate medication and dosage are identified [[5](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR5 "Jha MK, Trivedi MH. Personalized antidepressant selection and pathway to novel treatments: clinical utility of targeting inflammation. Int J Mol Sci. 2018;19(1).")].

In recent years, while fewer new psychiatric medications have been introduced to the market, the number of medications labeled with pharmacogenomic information has been increasing [[6](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR6 "Lauschke VM, Zhou Y, Ingelman-Sundberg M. Novel genetic and epigenetic factors of importance for inter-individual differences in drug disposition, response and toxicity. Pharmacol Ther. 2019;197:122–52.")]. In the foreseeable future, advances in psychiatry and pharmacotherapy are likely to rely more on personalized treatments, with pharmacogenomics (PGx) being one of the key tools for such approaches. PGx studies have demonstrated that the efficacy of antidepressant medications is strongly influenced by genetic variants, particularly those related to genetic susceptibility to remission. Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have revealed significant heritability for remission, though not for the percentage improvement in symptoms, suggesting that certain genetic factors may affect the efficacy of antidepressants [[7](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR7 "Pain O, Hodgson K, Trubetskoy V, Ripke S, Marshe VS, Adams MJ, Byrne EM, Campos AI, Carrillo-Roa T, Cattaneo A, et al. Identifying the common genetic basis of antidepressant response. Biol Psychiatry Glob Open Sci. 2022;2(2):115–26.")]. Thus, pharmacogenomics provides new directions for optimizing antidepressant therapy, helping to predict drug responses, reduce adverse effects, and ultimately enhance treatment efficacy and patient quality of life.

## Case presentation

A 27-year-old woman has been diagnosed with recurrent depression. Her laboratory tests and physical findings were all within normal limits, including routine blood work, full biochemistry, thyroid axis hormones, syphilis and acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS) tests, electrocardiogram, cardiac ultrasound, electroencephalogram, abdominal ultrasound, and cranial computed tomography (CT).

Three depressive episodes have been a part of her life. The symptoms of the first and second depressive episodes were symptomatically controlled after treatment with antidepressants. However, during the 3rd depressive episode, the patient experienced uncontrollable depression and severe insomnia, despite trying multiple antidepressant regimens. Additionally, her condition continued to deteriorate, leading to the development of severe anxiety and discomfort symptoms such as fidgeting and palpitations. The patient also experienced Lorazepam tolerance and withdrawal problems.

During her first and second episodes of depression, she typically chooses to purchase and take psychiatric medications independently, as it is inconvenient for her to go to the hospital and difficult for her to follow the prescribed medication schedule. Due to the lack of medical records, some of the medications she took on her own can no longer be traced in terms of their names or dosages. The patient was in good physical health and had no somatic illnesses. The three depressive episodes were treated with medication only, with no use of psychotherapy or physical therapy.

Figure [1](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#Fig1) illustrates the patient’s past medically documented medication use, and Table [1](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#Tab1) provides details on the medication used during the 3rd depressive episode.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs12888-025-06481-4/MediaObjects/12888_2025_6481_Fig1_HTML.png)

Previous drug use

The patient opted for pharmacogenetic testing due to poor responses to empirical medications and the development of Lorazepam resistance and withdrawal issues. DNA samples were collected via fingertip blood and analyzed by a third-party medical testing organization. Doctors assessed the existing treatment regimen in conjunction with the PGx test results and adjusted the treatment plan accordingly.

The patient’s cytochrome P450 (CYP) 2C19 genotype indicates intermediate metabolism, while CYP 2D6 suggests extensive metabolism. Since citalopram is primarily metabolized by CYP 2C19 and CYP 3A4, with a minimal amount metabolized by CYP 2D6 [[8](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR8 "Bain KT, Knowlton CH, Turgeon J. Medication risk mitigation: coordinating and collaborating with health care systems, universities, and researchers to facilitate the design and execution of practice-based research. Clin Geriatr Med. 2017;33(2):257–81.")], individuals with intermediate CYP 2C19 metabolism may have reduced enzyme activity, leading to decreased citalopram clearance and an increased risk of toxicity. During the course of treatment, although the dose of citalopram was adjusted to the recommended 40 mg/day, the patient’s symptoms did not improve effectively and worsened after 6 weeks, leading to mistrust of the medication and ultimately prompting a change.

The PGx test recommended treating this patient with fluvoxamine, fluoxetine, paroxetine, vortioxetine, or mianserin. After evaluating the patient’s symptoms and referring to the Class A recommended medications in the *Chinese Guideline for Major Depressive Disorder* (second edition), the doctor decided to prescribe fluvoxamine. This choice was made considering its potential to improve the patient’s anxiety, depression, and sleep problems.

The patient is currently taking agomelatine, which is primarily metabolized by CYP 1A2 (accounting for approximately 90% of its metabolism). CYP 2C9 and CYP 2C19 isoenzymes also play a role in the metabolism of agomelatine, though to a lesser extent [[9](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR9 "Englisch S, Jung HS, Lewien A, Becker A, Nowak U, Braun H, Eisenacher S, Thiem J, Meyer-Lindenberg A, Zink M. Response to agomelatine treatment is independent of smoking status and dosage: results from the AGOPSYCH study. Pharmacopsychiatry. 2019;52(3):142–6.")]. Fluvoxamine, a potent inhibitor of CYP 1A2 and a moderate inhibitor of CYP 2C9, significantly reduces the metabolism of agomelatine [[10](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR10 "Luong TT, Powers CN, Reinhardt BJ, Weina PJ. Pre-clinical drug-drug interactions (DDIs) of gefitinib with/without losartan and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs): citalopram, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, paroxetine, sertraline, and venlafaxine. Curr Res Pharmacol Drug Discov. 2022;3:100112.")]. The combination of these two drugs slows the elimination of agomelatine from the body, leading to increased blood levels. Furthermore, the agomelatine drug insert clearly states that it should not be used in combination with fluvoxamine, necessitating the discontinuation of agomelatine before starting fluvoxamine treatment.

The patient is currently taking quetiapine to benefit from its sedative effects to aid sleep and its role as an emotional stabilizer. Quetiapine is primarily metabolized by CYP 3A4, which is responsible for approximately 75% of its clearance, converting quetiapine into N-desalkylquetiapine [[11](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR11 "Zubiaur P, Fernández-Campos P, Navares-Gómez M, Soria-Chacartegui P, Villapalos-García G, Román M, Mejía-Abril G, Ochoa D, Abad-Santos F. Variants in COMT, CYP3A5, CYP2B6, and ABCG2 alter quetiapine pharmacokinetics. Pharmaceutics. 2021;13(10).")]. Additionally, CYP 2D6 and CYP 3A5 contribute to its metabolism [[12](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR12 "DeVane CL, Nemeroff CB. Clinical pharmacokinetics of quetiapine: an atypical antipsychotic. Clin Pharmacokinet. 2001;40(7):509–22."), [13](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR13 "Bakken GV, Rudberg I, Christensen H, Molden E, Refsum H, Hermann M. Metabolism of quetiapine by CYP3A4 and CYP3A5 in presence or absence of cytochrome B5. Drug Metab Dispos. 2009;37(2):254–8.")]. Fluvoxamine, being a moderate inhibitor of CYP 3A4, reduces the clearance rate of quetiapine in the body, leading to an increase in its blood concentration. This elevation poses a risk of toxicity, so it’s essential to consider the quetiapine dose when using the two drugs in combination.

Patients frequently misuse sedative-hypnotics, leading to the development of tolerance and withdrawal issues with lorazepam. Consequently, a tapering-off approach for lorazepam is initiated. Given the patient’s severe insomnia and the approximately 2-week duration for fluvoxamine to take effect, the patient will continue using zopiclone and alprazolam to improve sleep. The plan includes a gradual tapering-off of alprazolam once the patient’s symptoms are under control.

In conducting the assessment and developing the treatment plan, the doctor considered several factors, including overall medication use (efficacy, adverse effects, drug interactions, tolerability), PGx test results, and the patient’s behavioral patterns and personality traits. Over the next 6 weeks, the doctor adjusted the medications according to the treatment plan, and the adjustment process is detailed in Fig. [2](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#Fig2). The patient’s anxiety is reduced, depression is better controlled, sleep improves, and she can function normally. The changes in patients’ scale scores before and after treatment are visually depicted in Fig. [3](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#Fig3).

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs12888-025-06481-4/MediaObjects/12888_2025_6481_Fig2_HTML.png)

Medication adjustment process

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs12888-025-06481-4/MediaObjects/12888_2025_6481_Fig3_HTML.png)

Illustrates the fluctuations in scale scores before and after treatment

## Discussion

Depression is one of the most common psychiatric disorders. Our patient had recurrent depressive disorder with poor outcomes despite conventional empirical treatment. A 2012 review study showed that patients who failed to respond adequately to antidepressant medications had healthcare-related expenditures that were approximately $10,000 more per year than those who responded effectively [[14](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR14 "Mrazek DA, Hornberger JC, Altar CA, Degtiar I. A review of the clinical, economic, and societal burden of treatment-resistant depression: 1996–2013. Psychiatr Serv. 2014;65(8):977–87.")].

Although metabolic phenotype is an important factor in guiding antidepressant selection, patient response to antidepressants is not entirely dependent on metabolic rate. We believe that medication adjustment should consider a combination of clinical symptoms, patient feedback, and individual drug response. In our patient, despite being an intermediate metabolizer of CYP 2C19 and having unmeasured citalopram blood levels, symptoms were not effectively controlled. Clinical experience has shown that drug efficacy, side effects, and subjective patient feedback also play key roles in clinical decision-making.

Genetic differences determine the expression of drug-metabolizing enzymes, drug transporter proteins, and receptors, which in turn affect drug metabolism and action [[15](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR15 "Brown L, Vranjkovic O, Li J, Yu K, Al Habbab T, Johnson H, Brown K, Jablonski MR, Dechairo B. The clinical utility of combinatorial pharmacogenomic testing for patients with depression: a meta-analysis. Pharmacogenomics. 2020;21(8):559–69.")]. A meta-analysis of single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) showed that about 20–40% of the differences in antidepressant response can be attributed to common genetic variants [[7](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR7 "Pain O, Hodgson K, Trubetskoy V, Ripke S, Marshe VS, Adams MJ, Byrne EM, Campos AI, Carrillo-Roa T, Cattaneo A, et al. Identifying the common genetic basis of antidepressant response. Biol Psychiatry Glob Open Sci. 2022;2(2):115–26.")]. Differences in response to certain drugs may even be explained by genetic factors up to 95% [[16](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR16 "Eichelbaum M, Ingelman-Sundberg M, Evans WE. Pharmacogenomics and individualized drug therapy. Annu Rev Med. 2006;57:119–37.")]. Genetic variants closely related to drug metabolism are primarily found in phase I metabolism, particularly CYP 2C19 and CYP 2D6 gene polymorphisms [[17](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR17 "van Westrhenen R, Aitchison KJ, Ingelman-Sundberg M, Jukić MM. Pharmacogenomics of antidepressant and antipsychotic treatment: how far have we got and where are we going? Front Psychiatry. 2020;11:94."), [18](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR18 "Kirchheiner J, Nickchen K, Bauer M, Wong ML, Licinio J, Roots I, Brockmöller J. Pharmacogenetics of antidepressants and antipsychotics: the contribution of allelic variations to the phenotype of drug response. Mol Psychiatry. 2004;9(5):442–73.")]. These polymorphisms affect the pharmacokinetic parameters of drugs (e.g., clearance, half-life, and blood concentration), which in turn increases the risk of adverse effects and influences therapeutic efficacy [[19](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR19 "Seeringer A, Kirchheiner J. Pharmacogenetics-guided dose modifications of antidepressants. Clin Lab Med. 2008;28(4):619–26.")].

Typically, based on genetic makeup, populations are categorized into ultra-rapid metabolizers, extensive metabolizers, intermediate metabolizers, and poor metabolizers. Poor metabolizers have slow drug clearance and are more prone to adverse effects. Ultra-rapid metabolizers, on the other hand, have higher metabolic rates, and the drug may fail to reach therapeutic concentrations, resulting in reduced efficacy. Extensive metabolizers have normal metabolic activity, with drug metabolism rates within the typical range [[20](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR20 "Caudle KE, Sangkuhl K, Whirl-Carrillo M, Swen JJ, Haidar CE, Klein TE, Gammal RS, Relling MV, Scott SA, Hertz DL, et al. Standardizing CYP2D6 genotype to phenotype translation: consensus recommendations from the Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium and Dutch Pharmacogenetics Working Group. Clin Transl Sci. 2020;13(1):116–24.")]. CYP 2D6 and CYP 2C19 are highly polymorphic enzymes involved in the metabolism of approximately 20% and 5–10% of drugs, including selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs), and others, respectively [[21](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR21 "Caudle KE, Dunnenberger HM, Freimuth RR, Peterson JF, Burlison JD, Whirl-Carrillo M, Scott SA, Rehm HL, Williams MS, Klein TE, et al. Standardizing terms for clinical pharmacogenetic test results: consensus terms from the Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium (CPIC). Genet Med. 2017;19(2):215–23.")].

In our patient, CYP 2C19 was an intermediate metabolizer and CYP 2D6 was an extensive metabolizer. This metabolic combination may have led to decreased clearance of citalopram, increasing the risk of drug toxicity and potentially compromising therapeutic efficacy. Although changes in CYP 1A2 activity may have little to do with genetic factors, its phenotypic analysis can help in understanding certain drug interactions [[22](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR22 "Deardorff OGJ, Leanard V. Making sense of CYP2D6 and CYP1A2 genotype vs phenotype. Curr Psychiatry. 2018;17:41–5.")]. For example, CYP 1A2 metabolizes about 90% of agomelatine, and individuals with hyper inducible CYP 1A2 types (e.g., \*1F or \*1B carriers) have faster metabolism rates and lower drug concentrations [[23](/articles/10.1186/s12888-025-06481-4#ref-CR23 "Saiz-Rodríguez M, Ochoa D, Belmonte C, Román M, Vieira de Lara D, Zubiaur P, Koller D, Mejía G, Abad-Santos F. Polymorphisms in CYP1A2, CYP2C9 and ABCB1 affect agomelatine pharmacokinetics. J Psychopharmacol. 2019;33(4):522–31.")]. The present patient was a CYP 1A2 ultra-rapid metabolizer, which may have led to accelerated clearance of agomelatine and reduced its efficacy.

While genotyping provides useful information on metabolic potential, it is important to emphasize that metabolic activity should ideally be empirically confirmed, as enzymatic activity does not always directly correlate with the genotypes observed. Genetic variations, interactions between multiple alleles, and environmental factors may lead to discrepancies between genotype and observed enzymatic activity. This highlights the need for direct measurement of drug concentrations or phenotypic testing to assess true metabolic capacity.

In addition to metabolic phenotype and genetic background, drug dependence and withdrawal symptoms may also affect the therapeutic response. Specifically, in our patient, abrupt withdrawal after long-term lorazepam use may have led to withdrawal symptoms that overlapped with depression symptoms, such as anxiety, irritability, and mood swings. Considering that the patient had experienced lorazepam withdrawal symptoms during depressive episodes, some of her symptoms may be related to withdrawal reactions rather than purely underlying psychiatric disorders. Therefore, it remains uncertain whether the observed improvement in symptoms was due to the alleviation of withdrawal symptoms or to the effectiveness of the treatment. This factor requires further research and monitoring to clarify its impact on treatment outcomes.

This study is based on a single case, and although it provides an in-depth analysis of the patient’s treatment course, its results have limitations when generalized to a broader patient population. Individual differences may lead to variations in treatment outcomes, so these findings cannot be directly applied to other patients, especially those with different disease backgrounds or clinical variables. Nevertheless, this case provides valuable data on treatment outcomes in a specific patient population and offers initial guidance for future clinical studies and treatment strategies.

Although PGx-directed therapy offers new possibilities for individualized treatment and the ability to optimize therapeutic decisions based on genetic information to maximize efficacy and minimize adverse effects—particularly in patients with genetic variants known to affect drug metabolism—PGx-directed therapy still has its limitations. While genetic data can provide valuable information about drug response, individual differences can still affect treatment outcomes. Environmental factors, co-morbid conditions, and non-genetic factors may cause patients to exhibit different treatment responses even when genetically guided. Therefore, although genetic information is crucial in guiding treatment, it does not fully explain all treatment responses in patients. The differences in treatment response in this patient, even after receiving PGx guidance, suggest the need for ongoing clinical monitoring and individualized adjustment.

In conclusion, although PGx-directed therapy is a major advance in individualized medicine, it is not a panacea. A balance of genetic, clinical, and environmental factors is still needed to achieve optimal therapeutic outcomes. Additionally, the lack of specialized facilities or well-trained professionals in some regions limits the widespread use of PGx testing, and the cost of PGx testing is also a significant obstacle to its broader application.

## Conclusions

Recognizing the complexity of antidepressant response, our patients seek to enhance their understanding and that of other healthcare providers through PGx testing to bolster the credibility of their medication choices. While PGx is just one aspect considered in selecting a treatment regimen for depression, it remains a valuable tool to increase credibility and mitigate potential adverse events. By considering individual differences in genes associated with drug action, efforts are being made to achieve more precise treatment.

Highlights
----------

*   •Pharmacogenomic testing is available to guide the [treatment of depression](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/treatment-of-depression "Learn more about treatment of depression from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). 
*   •Pharmacogenomic guided medication enhances remission and response rates. 
*   •Pharmacogenomic guided medication reduces the occurrence of adverse effects. 

Abstract
--------

### Background

[Pharmacotherapy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/pharmacotherapy "Learn more about Pharmacotherapy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is one of the primary treatment modalities for depression. However, there is considerable variability in the individual response to [antidepressant medications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/antidepressant-medication "Learn more about antidepressant medications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Personalized medicine guided by [pharmacogenomic testing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/pharmacogenetic-testing "Learn more about pharmacogenomic testing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") may hold promise in addressing this issue.

### Methods

In this study, 665 depressive patients were randomly enrolled into two groups: the [pharmacogenomic testing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/pharmacogenetic-testing "Learn more about pharmacogenomic testing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") group (_n_=333) and the control group (_n_=332). In the testing group, participants underwent [pharmacogenomic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/pharmacogenomics "Learn more about pharmacogenomic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") testing, and clinicians customized the treatment plan with the result, while the control group relied solely on clinicians' experience. The primary outcomes were the proportion of remission and response, assessed with [Hamilton Depression Rating Scale](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/hamilton-rating-scale-for-depression "Learn more about Hamilton Depression Rating Scale from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (HDRS). The secondary outcomes included changes in HDRS scores over time and frequency of [adverse drug reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/adverse-drug-reaction "Learn more about adverse drug reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by the participants.

### Results

At week 8, the pharmacogenomic testing group showed significantly higher [remission rates](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/spontaneous-remission "Learn more about remission rates from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (24.0% v.s. 15.1%; RR=1.117; _P_=0.007) and response rates (39.3% v.s. 25.7%; RR=1.225; _P_<0.001) compared to the control group. By week 12, the pharmacogenomic testing group continued to demonstrate significant advantages in remission (31.0% v.s. 20.0%; RR=1.159; _P_=0.003) and response (48.7% v.s. 37.3%; RR=1.224; _P_=0.006). Additionally, [adverse drug reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/adverse-drug-reaction "Learn more about adverse drug reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") were less frequent in the pharmacogenomic testing group.

### Limitations

This study is not blind to clinicians and it's a single-center study.

Conclusions: Pharmacogenomic testing-guided drug therapy can provide greater assistance in the [treatment of depression](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/treatment-of-depression "Learn more about treatment of depression from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

1. Introduction
---------------

Depression poses a substantial [disease burden](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/disease-burden "Learn more about disease burden from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), significantly impacting the lives and overall [quality of life](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/quality-of-life "Learn more about quality of life from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of patients ([Xiang et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0130)), with the potential to lead to severe functional impairments or even death ([Wang et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0120)). Unfortunately, it is regrettable that 30% to 40% of patients do not respond well to [antidepressant medications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/antidepressant-medication "Learn more about antidepressant medications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and the treatment for patients is often severely hindered by the [side effects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/side-effect "Learn more about side effects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the drugs, which may also lead to a deterioration in the effectiveness of the treatment. This variability is largely attributed to [genetic factors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetic-determinism "Learn more about genetic factors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Sullivan and Geschwind, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0100)).

Currently, the diagnostic criteria for depression are primarily based on [symptomatology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/symptomatology "Learn more about symptomatology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Moreover, the selection of treatment plans for depression relies heavily on the individual clinical experience of clinicians, which largely depends on subjective assessments, lacking objective evidence to support the formulation of treatment strategies. Therefore, pharmacogenomic testing has emerged as a promising diagnostic tool, enabling clinicians to guide the selection and dosage of antidepressant medications. The application of pharmacogenomic testing may offer improved drug selection strategies for patients with [genetic alterations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/gene-mutation "Learn more about genetic alterations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") impacting drug-related genes ([Corponi et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0045); [van Schaik et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0090)).

Pharmacological treatment is the most common choice for patients with depression. However, there is substantial variability in the individual response to medication within this patient population ([Seyhan and Carini, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0095)). One of the reasons for this variability is that genetic variations can act as important variables that influence drug [pharmacokinetics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/pharmacokinetics "Learn more about pharmacokinetics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or [pharmacodynamics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/pharmacodynamics "Learn more about pharmacodynamics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), thus affecting treatment efficacy. On the other hand, drug safety can also be influenced by gene-drug interactions, leading to [adverse effects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/adverse-event "Learn more about adverse effects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Zhou et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0140)). Therefore, personalized pharmacogenomic testing tailored to individual patients is of paramount importance.

Currently, most pharmacogenomic testing focuses on genes encoding the [cytochrome P450](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/cytochrome-p450 "Learn more about cytochrome P450 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CYP) [monooxygenase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/unspecific-monooxygenase "Learn more about monooxygenase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") system ([Muller et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0070); [Zanger and Schwab, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0135)). Research has revealed that genetic variations in [CYP2D6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/cyp2d6 "Learn more about CYP2D6 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [CYP2C19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/cyp2c19 "Learn more about CYP2C19 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [CYP2B6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cyp2b6 "Learn more about CYP2B6 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can impact the metabolism of numerous antidepressant medications, potentially affecting dosage, efficacy, and tolerance. Genotypes of SLC6A4 and HTR2A may also be associated with antidepressant drugs([Beunk et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0025); [Bousman et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0030); [Brouwer et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0035)). Variations related to the response to lithium have been identified on genes regulating the [glutamate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/glutamic-acid "Learn more about glutamate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") system, GADL1 and [GRIA2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/gria2 "Learn more about GRIA2 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which also hold significance for the treatment of depression([Vecera et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0110)). The UGT2B15 genotype has been found to be linked to the metabolism of [lorazepam](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/lorazepam "Learn more about lorazepam from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), providing guidance for addressing insomnia symptoms in depressive patients ([Dzierba et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165032724007997#bb0055)).This indicates that the medication regimen selected based on the results of pharmacogenomic testing could potentially enhance the treatment effectiveness of patients.

This study employed a clinical randomized controlled design to test two research hypotheses: (1) Pharmacogenomics-guided treatment in the intervention group would lead to greater rates of remission and response. (2) Pharmacogenomics-guided treatment in the intervention group would result in fewer [adverse drug reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/adverse-drug-reaction "Learn more about adverse drug reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

2. Material and methods
-----------------------

### 2.1. Interventions

On the day of baseline, the participants were randomly assigned to either the pharmacogenomic testing group or the control group at a 1:1 ratio. When the participants were assigned to the testing group, they underwent collection of oral mucosal epithelial cells for pharmacogenomic testing. All the participants received treatment at the outpatient clinic of [psychiatry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/psychiatry "Learn more about psychiatry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") department, Second Affiliated Hospital of Zhejiang University, School of Medicine. In pharmacogenomic testing group, the clinicians were required to consider the participants' symptom characteristics, their own clinical experience, and refer to the results of pharmacogenomic testing to guide the medication treatment. While in control group, participants did not undergo pharmacogenomic testing, and the clinicians relied on their own clinical experience and the participants' symptom characteristics to guide the medication treatment.

The study protocol was approved by the Ethics Review Committee of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Zhejiang University and the [informed consent](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/informed-consent "Learn more about informed consent from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was obtained from all subjects.

### 2.2. Participants

A total of 665 participants with depressive disorders, who were admitted to the outpatient department of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Zhejiang University School of Medicine, from July 15, 2020, to March 30, 2023, were enrolled as the study subjects. The inclusion criteria were as follows: 1) Diagnosis of depressive disorder according to the diagnostic criteria of DSM-5 by two or more senior psychiatrists; 2) Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HDRS) score >17; 3) Age between 14 and 75 years old; 4) At least a junior high school education or higher, capable of completing the scale assessment. The exclusion criteria were as follows: 1) Presence of other [mental disorders](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/mental-disorder "Learn more about mental disorders from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (including [bipolar disorder](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/bipolar-disorder "Learn more about bipolar disorder from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [schizophrenia](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/dementia-praecox "Learn more about schizophrenia from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [schizoaffective disorder](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/schizoaffective-psychosis "Learn more about schizoaffective disorder from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), substance addiction, [intellectual disability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/intellectual-impairment "Learn more about intellectual disability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), anxiety disorders, feeding and eating disorders, personality disorders, etc.); 2) Presence of significant [central nervous system diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/central-nervous-system-disease "Learn more about central nervous system diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (including history of [head trauma](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/closed-head-injury "Learn more about head trauma from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or infection, [intracranial tumors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/intracranial-tumor "Learn more about intracranial tumors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [cerebrovascular diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cerebrovascular-disease "Learn more about cerebrovascular diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), epilepsy, etc.); 3) History of significant [physical diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/physical-disease "Learn more about physical diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (malignant tumors, [myocardial infarction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/myocardial-infarction "Learn more about myocardial infarction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), liver or [kidney dysfunction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/kidney-dysfunction "Learn more about kidney dysfunction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), etc.); 4) History of abuse of sedatives, alcohol, or other psychoactive substances; 5) Received [electroconvulsive therapy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/electroconvulsive-therapy "Learn more about electroconvulsive therapy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or [transcranial magnetic stimulation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/transcranial-magnetic-stimulation "Learn more about transcranial magnetic stimulation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the past year; 6) Pregnant or breastfeeding women; 7) Abnormal results in electrocardiogram, [blood count](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/blood-cell-count "Learn more about blood count from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), liver function, [kidney function](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/kidney-function "Learn more about kidney function from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), or [thyroid function tests](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/thyroid-function-test "Learn more about thyroid function tests from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Both the participants and the clinicians will not receive any compensation for participating in the study.

### 2.3. Pharmacogenomic testing

participants in the pharmacogenomic testing group were subjected to immediate collection of oral epithelial cells upon enrollment. After sample collection, experimental personnel performed [nucleic acid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/nucleic-acid "Learn more about nucleic acid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") extraction, followed by amplification of the [nucleic acid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/nucleic-acid "Learn more about nucleic acid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") using the PCR method. Subsequently, [capillary electrophoresis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/capillary-electrophoresis "Learn more about capillary electrophoresis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was employed for further processing, and the results were analyzed using a [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") analyzer (Nanjing Suyuan CLASSIC 116 Genetic Analyzer).

This study conducted testing on a combination of [pharmacogenetic variants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/pharmacogenetic-variant "Learn more about pharmacogenetic variants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") related to [pharmacodynamics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/pharmacodynamics "Learn more about pharmacodynamics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [pharmacokinetics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/pharmacokinetics "Learn more about pharmacokinetics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The gene selection for this testing method was based on references from the Pharmacogenomics Knowledge [Base](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/base "Learn more about Base from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PharmGKB), as well as the Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium (CPIC) and the Dutch Pharmacogenetics Working Group (DPWG) guidelines, as detailed in Supplementary File, Table S1.

### 2.4. Medication options

Pharmacogenomics testing resulted in a personalized genetic testing report for each patient. The reports classified commonly used antidepressants into the following three categories: Cluster A (normal function of metabolism, good drug response, and low risk of adverse reactions); Cluster B (accelerated or slowed drug metabolism, suboptimal drug response); Cluster C (significantly slowed drug response, suboptimal drug response, or high risk of adverse reactions).

Clinicians chose appropriate medications based on the report. Clinicians first chose cluster A drugs, and if Cluster A did not have recommended drug, then chose cluster B, and so on. All pharmacogenomic testing group participants in this study had at least cluster B recommended drugs. The medication option for the combined use of antidepressants followed the principles as mentioned above. The use of [benzodiazepines](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/benzodiazepine "Learn more about benzodiazepines from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [mood stabilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/mood-stabilizer "Learn more about mood stabilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was allowed in the study.

### 2.5. Assessments

Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HDRS-24) was used to assess the severity of depressive symptoms and the progress of disease recovery in participants. Treatment Emergent Symptom Scale (TESS) was used to evaluate the occurrence of adverse drug reactions following the treatment. The assessments were conducted by trained psychological evaluators who were blinded to treatment at 4, 8, and 12 weeks after randomization and grouping. Electrocardiogram, complete blood count, liver function and kidney function data were collected at each visit. All the assessors underwent standardized training to ensure consistency and minimize errors during the data collection process.

# Pharmacogenomic testing for antidepressant treatment selection: lessons learned and roadmap forward

[*Neuropsychopharmacology*](/npp)
**volume 49**, pages 282–284 (2024)[Cite this article](#citeas)

9081 Accesses

9 Citations

### Subjects

## Abstract

Pharmacogenomic technology is a developing field with enthusiastic interest and broad application potential. Three large, controlled studies have been published exploring the benefit of pharmacogenomically guided antidepressant treatment selection. Though all three studies did not show significant benefit of using this technology, these studies laid the foundation for further research that should address the limitations of this previous research and currently available commercial platforms. Future research needs to include large scale pharmacogenomic trials with GWAS analytics across diverse groups with attention to cost-effectiveness models, particularly for cases of treatment resistance and polypharmacy. The application of results from these large scale pharmacogenomic trials must also include exploring optimal EHR user interface design.

## Introduction

Pharmacogenomic decision support tools are an evolving technology that help clinicians make informed decisions, based on clinically actionable genetic variants in pharmacokinetic (PK) and or pharmacodynamic (PD) genes that potentially impact safety, tolerability, and response to medications. The potential value add to the index episode of care is treatment selection based on the patient’s biology, not solely relying on FDA indication or off label clinical experience. Most commercially available platforms span multiple PK and PD genes and multiple classes of psychotropic drugs (antidepressant, antipsychotics, stimulants, mood stabilizers). However, antidepressant selection for treatment of major depressive disorder has been the area of most focused research in psychiatry.

## Major studies in the field

The National Network of Depression Center’s GUIDED Trial (Genomics Used to Improve Depression Decisions) enrolled 1167 outpatients in an 8-week, randomized, patient- and rater-blind controlled study of clinician *GUIDED* antidepressant treatment selection vs treatment as usual (TAU) [[1](/articles/s41386-023-01667-4#ref-CR1 "Greden JF, Parikh SV, Rothschild AJ, Thase ME, Dunlop BW, DeBattista C, et al. Impact of pharmacogenomics on clinical outcomes in major depressive disorder in the GUIDED trial: A large, patient- and rater-blinded, randomized, controlled study. J Psychiatr Res. 2019;111:59–67. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpsychires.2019.01.003
                  
                .")]. There was no difference in the primary outcome measure of mean change in Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (27.2% vs 24.4%, *p* = 0.107) for those participants who completed the study through week 8. Although the utility of secondary outcome measures in the context of a failed primary outcomes measure is of questionable validity [[2](/articles/s41386-023-01667-4#ref-CR2 "Andrade C. The Primary Outcome Measure and Its Importance in Clinical Trials. J Clin Psychiatry. 2015;76:1320–3.")], secondary outcome measures of response (26% vs 20%, *p* = 0.013) and remission (15% vs 10%, *p* = 0.007) favored GUIDED.

The PRIME CARE Randomized Trial enrolled 1944 patients receiving care at VA medical centers in a 24-week randomized open-label trial of pharmacogenomic guided treatment selection (*n* = 966) vs TAU (*n* = 978) [[3](/articles/s41386-023-01667-4#ref-CR3 "Oslin DW, Lynch KG, Shih MC, Ingram EP, Wray LO, Chapman SR, et al. Effect of Pharmacogenomic Testing for Drug-Gene Interactions on Medication Selection and Remission of Symptoms in Major Depressive Disorder: The PRIME Care Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA. 2022;328:151–61. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2022.9805
                  
                .")]. Educational materials were provided for test interpretation and consideration for prescribing low risk medications. The co-primary outcome measures were the proportion of prescriptions with a predicted drug-gene interaction written and remission of depression (PHQ-9 ≤ 5). There was a significant difference with the pharmacogenomic-guided group as they were more likely to receive a medication with a lower potential drug-gene interaction (no drug-gene vs moderate/substantial interaction). Remission rates over 24 weeks were significantly higher for pharmacogenomic care vs TAU, but not significantly higher at week 24. Perlis et al. (2020) conducted a randomized clinical trial of 304 patients with major depression and found no benefit of pharmacogenomic testing [[4](/articles/s41386-023-01667-4#ref-CR4 "Perlis RH, Dowd D, Fava M, Lencz T, Krause D. Randomized, Controlled, Participant- and Rater-blind trial of Pharmacogenomic Test-guided Treatment Versus Treatment As Usual for Major Depressive Disorder. Depression Anxiety. 2020;37:834–41. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/da.23029
                  
                .")]. Finally, the first randomized pharmacogenomic testing of depression treatment in adolescents (*n* = 176) found no significant difference in symptom improvement or side effect burden in the pharmacogenomic vs TAU groups [[5](/articles/s41386-023-01667-4#ref-CR5 "Vande Voort JL, Orth SS, Shekunov J, Romanowicz M, Geske JR, Ward JA, et al. A Randomized Controlled Trial of Combinatorial Pharmacogenetics Testing in Adolescent Depression. J Am Acad Child Adolesc Psychiatry. 2022;61:46–55. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaac.2021.03.011
                  
                .")]. Thus, a comprehensive review concluded that at present time, there is little evidence to support routine use of currently available pharmacogenomic tests in predicting antidepressant response [[6](/articles/s41386-023-01667-4#ref-CR6 "Goldberg JF, Nemeroff CB. Pharmacogenomics and antidepressants: efficacy and adverse drug reactions. In: Psychiatric Genomics (Tsermpini E, Alda M, Patrinos GP, Editors. Academic Press; 2022, p. 189–202.")].

## Limitation of current commercial platforms and study design

These large, controlled studies of pharmacogenomic testing for antidepressant treatment have not shown significant benefit of this decision support technology. It is important to emphasize these investigations were not designed as placebo-controlled investigational studies such as would be anticipated for a submission, for example, to the FDA for a new drug application. Future trial design should review the merits of a “placebo like” arm, but also randomization allocation, not only on treatment resistance and comorbidity, but metabolic capacity, recognizing both P450 metabolizer genotype and additional drugs that further induce or inhibit cytochrome metabolism can impact study results [[7](/articles/s41386-023-01667-4#ref-CR7 "Zeier Z, Carpenter LL, Kalin NH, Rodriguez CI, McDonald WM, Widge AS, et al. Clinical Implementation of Pharmacogenetic Decision Support Tools for Antidepressant Drug Prescribing. Am J Psychiatry. 2018;175:873–86. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1176/appi.ajp.2018.17111282
                  
                .")].

While the primary outcome measure of GUIDED Trial was not met, important exploratory analyses have made a positive impact on future trial design. For example, 912 participants (439 GUIDED, 473 TAU) identified from the original intent to treat cohort at baseline, had a clinically actionable variant or gene-drug interaction categorized as “use with caution” or “used with increased caution and with more frequent monitoring” [[8](/articles/s41386-023-01667-4#ref-CR8 "Thase ME, Parikh SV, Rothschild AJ, Dunlop BW, DeBattista C, Conway CR, et al. Impact of Pharmacogenomics on Clinical Outcomes for Patients Taking Medications With Gene-Drug Interactions in a Randomized Controlled Trial. J Clin Psychiatry. 2019;80:19m12910. 
                  https://doi.org/10.4088/JCP.19m12910
                  
                .")]. Excluding treatment selection that did not pose a gene-drug interaction (i.e., no additional value of genotyping based on treatment selection), GUIDED participants had greater mean change in HDRS than TAU (27% vs 22% *p* = 0.029) as well as response (27% vs 19%, *p* = 0.008) and remission 18% vs 11%, *p* = 0.003). A second exploratory analysis evaluated if the Hamilton-D6, which focused on core symptoms of depression, would be more sensitive to change when exploring a potential biologic variable related to outcome [[9](/articles/s41386-023-01667-4#ref-CR9 "Dunlop BW, Parikh SV, Rothschild AJ, Thase ME, DeBattista C, Conway CR, et al. Comparing sensitivity to change using the 6-item versus the 17-item Hamilton depression rating scale in the GUIDED randomized controlled trial. BMC Psychiatry. 2019;19:420. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1186/s12888-019-2410-2
                  
                .")]. The HAM-D6 as a continuous measure demonstrated significantly greater percentage decrease at week 8 in the GUIDED group (4.4%, *p* = 0.023); this difference was not present when using the full HDRS (3.2%, *p* = 0.069).

One could argue, given the substantial number of pharmacokinetic (PK) and pharmacodynamic genetic variants, as well as varied treatment selection (GUIDED included 21 antidepressants and 7 antipsychotics, PRIME CARE included 1406 participants who received an antidepressant), these studies were likely underpowered to identify a significant treatment arm difference. One approach to reduce this heterogeneity would be to consider a narrower group of variants or treatment(s), studying biotransformation (PK) and dynamic mechanism of drug action (PD) together. For example, a secondary analysis completed from the STAR\*D study focused on depressed participants who prospectively did not achieve remission from the SSRI citalopram and were subsequently enrolled in an SNRI trial of venlafaxine [[10](/articles/s41386-023-01667-4#ref-CR10 "Ahmed AT, Biernacka JM, Jenkins G, Rush AJ, Shinozaki G, Veldic M, et al. Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic interaction associated with venlafaxine-XR remission in patients with major depressive disorder with history of citalopram/escitalopram treatment failure. J Affect Disord. 2019;246:62–8. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jad.2018.12.021
                  
                .")]. Remission with venlafaxine, a 2D6 metabolized antidepressant, in prior prospective confirmation of non-remission with citalopram, a 2C19 metabolized antidepressant, was associated with a significant interaction between P450 2D6 ultra-rapid metabolizer phenotype and a greater amount of serotonin *SLC6A4 L/L* and noradrenergic *SLC6A2* transporter protein. Single drug studies targeting PK-PD interaction will have greater statistical power and clearly can provide potential clinical value in practice.

From a pharmacokinetic perspective, the bar or level of evidence for safety parameters, such as FDA black box warnings for 2D6 poor metabolizer phenotype and QT prolongation or reduced metabolic conversion of prodrug tamoxifen to chemotherapeutically active endoxifen, is significantly lower than a genetic variant that is a biomarker of treatment response. It is of note that pharmacokinetic variation has contributed to the newly FDA drug for depression treatment, dextromethorphan bupropion combination, both from the standpoint of mechanism of action, with bupropion inhibiting 2D6 increasing dextromethorphan (a NMDA antagonist) levels, as well as clinical guidelines with reduced dosing of the drug with known poor metabolizers at 2D6 (<https://www.axsome.com/auvelity-prescribing-information.pdf>). From a pharmacodynamic perspective, the black box warning of *HLA-B\*1502* variation and carbamazepine-associated Stevens-Johnsons syndrome has impacted clinical practice; further work is encouraged to explore potential PD variants related to drug safety. For example, a recent meta-analysis of 7 studies investigating antidepressant-associated treatment emergent mania (TEM) in bipolar disorder identified a significant association between the short or s allele of the serotonin transporter *SLC6A4* and TEM (OR:1.43; 95% CI:1.0–2.06; *p* = 0.05). An important commentary from the author underscored focusing on TEM biomarkers beyond serotonin transporter, highlighting no prior work with norepinephrine or dopamine transporters, and more broadly, but not part of commercial platforms today, genomewide association studies [[11](/articles/s41386-023-01667-4#ref-CR11 "Nuñez NA, Gardea-Resendez M, Melhuish Beaupre L, Coombes BJ, Sanchez Ruiz J, Ozerdem A, et al. Anti-depressant associated treatment emergent mania: evaluating pharmacogenomic targets of potential clinical value. J Clin Psychopharmacology. 2024, in press.")]. Indeed, there are several polymorphisms including the CRH binding protein [[12](/articles/s41386-023-01667-4#ref-CR12 "O’Connell CP, Goldstein-Piekarski AN, Nemeroff CB, Schatzberg AF, Debattista C, Carrillo-Roa T, et al. Antidepressant outcomes predicted by genetic variation in corticotropin-releasing hormone binding protein. Am J Psychiatry. 2018;175:251–61.")] and the ABCB1 gene [[13](/articles/s41386-023-01667-4#ref-CR13 "Schatzberg AF, DeBattista C, Lazzeroni LC, Etkin A, Murphy GM Jr, Williams LM. ABCB1 genetic effects on antidepressant outcomes: a report from the iSPOT trial. Am J Psychiatry. 2015;172:751–9.")].

## Conclusion

Pharmacogenomic testing is a technology that will continue to advance and inherently has potential to transform clinical practice. The roadmap to greater precision in current and future novel therapeutics should be with large scale pharmacogenomic trails employing GWAS analytics and cost-effectiveness models, in particular in cases of treatment resistance and polypharmacy. To address generalizability, future studies need to prioritize diverse groups to look at possible genetic variation by ethnic groups and optimal EHR user interface design. How genomic data is added to large scale databases focused on optimal EHR interface or conventional decision support tools is yet to be determined.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Pharmacogenomics aids in selecting optimal antidepressants by predicting patient responses, enhancing efficacy while minimizing side effects, ultimately personalizing treatment for depression.

Pharmacogenomics facilitates the identification of specific genetic mutations, enabling the use of targeted therapies that improve treatment outcomes and reduce unnecessary toxicity in cancer patients.
Genetic testing helps tailor cardiovascular medications, ensuring appropriate dosing and drug selection, thereby enhancing therapeutic effectiveness and reducing adverse cardiovascular events.
Pharmacogenomic insights allow clinicians to predict and mitigate potential adverse drug reactions, improving patient safety and treatment adherence through personalized medication strategies.
